entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the alchemist's coat of arms (frontispiece)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the alchemist's coat of arms (frontispiece).

## Iconography
A heraldic shield set within a painted architectural frame: the arms display a radiant golden sun — the *splendor solis*, the splendour of the Sun — that gives the treatise its name. Banderoles and a Latin or German motto frame the device, and grotesque, foliate and figural ornament crowds the border in the manner of a Renaissance armorial.

## Significance
The opening shield announces the whole programme: the Work aims at *Sol*, philosophical gold, the perfected solar tincture. By casting alchemy in the language of heraldry the manuscript dignifies the laboratory as a noble pursuit and binds the [[motif:solar|solar]] goal to lineage and honour. The arms are the emblematic 'title page' of one of the most beautiful of all alchemical books.

## For artists & game designers
A perfect arcane crest: a sun-in-splendour shield in an illuminated frame — drop-in heraldry for an alchemical order or guild.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the knight at the fountain (the philosopher)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the knight at the fountain (the philosopher).

## Iconography
An armoured knight or philosopher stands beside a flowing fountain, often holding a shield and a flask, with the spring of the *aqua permanens* (the permanent water, the philosophical mercury) welling at his feet. The scene is enclosed in the manuscript's signature painted architectural border teeming with figures, flora and fauna.

## Significance
The knight-philosopher guards the source of the alchemical water — the [[motif:mercury|mercurial]] spring from which the Work begins. He embodies the adept's vigilance and the chivalric framing of the quest for the Stone, a motif the *Splendor Solis* shares with the romance tradition. The fountain is the prima materia made to flow.

## For artists & game designers
An armoured guardian at a glowing spring — a ready quest-giver or threshold figure for an alchemical level.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the philosophical tree / two philosophers
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the philosophical tree / two philosophers.

## Iconography
Two robed philosophers confer before a tree or a fountain, one often presenting a golden vessel or pointing to a flask. The painted border frames them with putti, beasts and architectural fantasy.

## Significance
The conferring sages personify the transmission of secret knowledge — the Work is learned 'from mouth to mouth' between master and disciple. The tree is the [[motif:philosophical-tree|arbor philosophica]], whose growth images the maturing of metals toward gold. The plate sets the doctrinal frame before the operations begin.

## For artists & game designers
Two sages in debate beside a symbolic tree — instant lore-dialogue staging for a tutorial scene.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the drowning king rescued from the flood
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the drowning king rescued from the flood.

## Iconography
A crowned king flounders in water and is hauled to safety — a man on the bank reaches to draw him from the flood, set within the lavish architectural border.

## Significance
The drowning and rescue of the king is *solutio* and renewal: the old fixed body (gold, the king) must be 'killed' by dissolution in the philosophical water and then revived. It is one of the cycle's great images of death-and-rebirth, kin to the drowned kings of the *Rosarium* and *Aurora consurgens*. The flood is the mercurial bath that undoes and remakes the metallic body.

## For artists & game designers
A drowning crowned figure dragged from dark water — a dramatic rescue beat with built-in alchemical symbolism.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the dark king and the new-risen sun (sol niger / sunrise)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the dark king and the new-risen sun (sol niger / sunrise).

## Iconography
A landscape under a strange sun — in the cycle's celebrated leaf a swarthy or sable figure stands in a marsh beneath a darkened or newly risen sun, the *sol niger* of the blackening giving way to dawn.

## Significance
This is the *nigredo*, the black phase, where the matter putrefies before it can be reborn whitened and reddened. The black sun (*sol niger*) is among the most haunting emblems in all alchemy — the death of the old solar body that must precede the new light. The marshy, twilit setting renders putrefaction as landscape.

## For artists & game designers
The *sol niger* over a dead marsh — an iconic 'black sun' tableau for a corruption or underworld stage.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the queen (Luna) enthroned
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the queen (Luna) enthroned.

## Iconography
A crowned queen, often robed in white or silver and associated with the moon, sits or stands in state within the architectural frame.

## Significance
The Queen is *Luna*, the lunar feminine principle — the white tincture, the philosophical silver, partner to the solar King. Her appearance marks the turn toward the *albedo*, the whitening, when the washed matter shines like the moon. She is the bride awaiting the chymical wedding.

## For artists & game designers
A moon-crowned queen in silver — the lunar counterpart figure for a paired king-and-queen design.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the king (Sol) enthroned
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the king (Sol) enthroned.

## Iconography
A crowned king in red or gold majesty, associated with the sun, enthroned within the painted border.

## Significance
The King is *Sol*, the solar masculine principle — the red tincture, philosophical gold, the perfected fixed body that is the goal of the Work. His enthronement images the *rubedo*, the reddening, in which the Stone attains its royal, tinting power. King and Queen together are sulphur and mercury, the chymical bridegroom and bride.

## For artists & game designers
A sun-crowned king in royal red — the solar half of the alchemical royal pair.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the hermaphrodite (Rebis)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the hermaphrodite (Rebis).

## Iconography
A two-headed, dual-bodied figure — male on one side, female on the other — stands crowned in the border, often holding emblems of sun and moon.

## Significance
The *Rebis* ('two-thing') is the union of opposites: Sol and Luna, sulphur and mercury, fixed and volatile fused into a single androgynous body. It is the *coniunctio*, the chymical wedding consummated, and one of alchemy's master-images for wholeness reconciled out of duality. Jung read it as the symbol of the integrated self.

## For artists & game designers
The classic two-headed Rebis — an immediately legible 'union of opposites' icon for a transformation reward screen.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — flask: the planetary stages (a sun-and-moon vessel)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, flask: the planetary stages (a sun-and-moon vessel).

## Iconography
A great glass flask or round-bottomed vessel dominates the page, its bulb holding a miniature scene — in the cycle a sequence of seven such flasks carries the Work through its planetary stages, each globe a little painted world of sun, moon, birds or figures.

## Significance
The seven flasks of the *Splendor Solis* map the operation onto the seven planets and seven metals, the matter ripening from Saturn-lead to Sol-gold. Each globe is a sealed cosmos — the philosophical egg in which death, washing and rebirth unfold under the eye of a planetary ruler. This is the treatise's most original contribution to the alchemical picture-book.

## For artists & game designers
A round flask whose glass bulb frames a tiny self-contained world — a perfect 'snow-globe' device for a stage-select interface.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the peacock's tail flask (cauda pavonis)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the peacock's tail flask (cauda pavonis).

## Iconography
A flask whose bulb shines with iridescent, peacock-feather colours — or a peacock itself displayed within the vessel — set in the architectural border.

## Significance
The *cauda pavonis*, the peacock's tail, is the shimmering play of colours that breaks out on the matter between the blackening and the whitening: a sign that the Work is alive and turning. The all-at-once iridescence promises that every colour, and so the perfected tincture, is latent in the stone.

## For artists & game designers
An iridescent peacock-tail shimmer inside glass — a gorgeous 'phase-shift' visual cue for a brewing minigame.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the white queen / white stone flask
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the white queen / white stone flask.

## Iconography
A flask or scene dominated by whiteness — a lunar woman in white, or a vessel showing the matter washed to a silvery brightness — within the border.

## Significance
The *albedo* perfected: the matter, washed clean of its blackness, shines white as the moon, yielding the white tincture that transmutes base metal to silver. The White Queen is the purified lunar body, ready to be reddened into the King.

## For artists & game designers
A luminous all-white vessel — a clean 'purified' state-icon contrasting the earlier blackening.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the red king / red stone flask
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the red king / red stone flask.

## Iconography
A flask or scene dominated by red — a solar man in red, or a vessel showing the matter glowing crimson — within the architectural frame.

## Significance
The *rubedo*, the reddening: the white stone is driven on to red, the perfected solar tincture that ripens base metal into gold. The Red King is the culminating fixed body, the Philosophers' Stone in its tinting glory — the end toward which every prior plate has pointed.

## For artists & game designers
A glowing crimson vessel — the triumphant 'gold achieved' state for a climactic reveal.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the sun child (filius solis)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the sun child (filius solis).

## Iconography
A radiant infant or youth, crowned or haloed with solar rays, born within or rising from the vessel — framed by the painted border.

## Significance
The *filius philosophorum*, the child of the Sun, is the reborn Stone: the new solar body delivered from the death and washing of the matter. The motif of the divine child crowns the death-and-rebirth drama of the whole cycle — the gold reborn as a living, luminous offspring.

## For artists & game designers
A glowing newborn sun-child — a luminous 'birth of the prize' moment for a culmination cutscene.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the moon child (filius lunae)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the moon child (filius lunae).

## Iconography
A pale infant or youth associated with the moon, born within the vessel, set in the architectural border.

## Significance
The lunar child is the white stone reborn — the silvery counterpart to the solar child, the purified body delivered from the *albedo*. Paired with the sun-child, it images the twin perfections, white and red, silver and gold, that the Work seeks.

## For artists & game designers
A pale, moon-touched infant — the lunar twin to the sun-child for paired birth imagery.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the alchemists at work in the laboratory
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the alchemists at work in the laboratory.

## Iconography
Men labour at a furnace and bench in a workshop — tending the athanor, working bellows, handling flasks — within the manuscript's border. In the *Splendor Solis* such genre scenes appear among the later leaves as small painted vignettes.

## Significance
These laboratory scenes ground the soaring allegory in real practice: the patient *coction*, the watched fire, the long tending of the vessel. They are a reminder that the Work was as much sweat and craft as symbol — the 'ora et labora' of the bench. Such vignettes are prized as documents of the early-modern alchemical workshop.

## For artists & game designers
A working alchemical lab with furnace, bellows and glassware — reference-grade staging for a crafting room.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the toiling figures (children's games / labours)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the toiling figures (children's games / labours).

## Iconography
A bustling scene of ordinary people at work and play — in the cycle the later leaves famously include vignettes of children's games, washing, and daily labour beneath emblematic skies, all within the painted border.

## Significance
These genre 'labours' encode the alchemical operations in the guise of common toil: washing, playing, building. The conceit — that the highest secret is mirrored in the humblest daily work, 'women's work and child's play' — is a recurring alchemical topos, insisting the Stone is made by simple, patient means.

## For artists & game designers
Lively everyday-labour vignettes under a symbolic sky — ambient world-building for an alchemical town.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the citadel / the philosophers' city
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, the citadel / the philosophers' city.

## Iconography
A walled city or citadel rises in the background of a flask-scene or border — towers, gates and ramparts rendered with Renaissance precision.

## Significance
The philosophers' citadel images the completed, fortified Work — the Stone as a stronghold, the secret guarded behind walls. Architecture in the *Splendor Solis* is never idle: its painted buildings, like the elaborate borders themselves, frame the operations as a built, ordered cosmos achieved out of chaos.

## For artists & game designers
A Renaissance citadel as backdrop — a fortified 'secret city' vista for an alchemical realm.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — an illuminated leaf of the cycle (architectural border with allegory)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — depicting, in the cycle's programme, an illuminated leaf of the cycle (architectural border with allegory).

## Iconography
A leaf of the manuscript given over to the *Splendor Solis*'s celebrated painted frame: an architectural border alive with putti, beasts, flowers, grotesques and antique ornament enclosing a central allegorical scene or vessel.

## Significance
The borders are not decoration but argument — each frames its plate with a teeming microcosm, binding the central operation to the whole order of nature. The *Splendor Solis* borders rank among the finest manuscript illumination of the German Renaissance, and carry their own emblematic burden of birds, fruit and figures that gloss the Work within.

## For artists & game designers
A richly grotesque illuminated frame — a luxurious border template to enclose any alchemical plate or UI panel.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the alchemist's coat of arms (frontispiece).

## Iconography
A heraldic shield set within a painted architectural frame: the arms display a radiant golden sun — the *splendor solis*, the splendour of the Sun — that gives the treatise its name. Banderoles and a Latin or German motto frame the device, and grotesque, foliate and figural ornament crowds the border in the manner of a Renaissance armorial.

## Significance
The opening shield announces the whole programme: the Work aims at *Sol*, philosophical gold, the perfected solar tincture. By casting alchemy in the language of heraldry the manuscript dignifies the laboratory as a noble pursuit and binds the [[motif:solar|solar]] goal to lineage and honour. The arms are the emblematic 'title page' of one of the most beautiful of all alchemical books.

## For artists & game designers
A perfect arcane crest: a sun-in-splendour shield in an illuminated frame — drop-in heraldry for an alchemical order or guild.

entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the drowning king rescued from the flood.

## Iconography
A crowned king flounders in water and is hauled to safety — a man on the bank reaches to draw him from the flood, set within the lavish architectural border.

## Significance

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the dark king and the new-risen sun (sol niger / sunrise)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the dark king and the new-risen sun (sol niger / sunrise).

## Iconography
A landscape under a strange sun — in the cycle's celebrated leaf a swarthy or sable figure stands in a marsh beneath a darkened or newly risen sun, the *sol niger* of the blackening giving way to dawn.

## Significance
This is the *nigredo*, the black phase, where the matter putrefies before it can be reborn whitened and reddened. The black sun (*sol niger*) is among the most haunting emblems in all alchemy — the death of the old solar body that must precede the new light. The marshy, twilit setting renders putrefaction as landscape.

## For artists & game designers
The *sol niger* over a dead marsh — an iconic 'black sun' tableau for a corruption or underworld stage.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the queen (Luna) enthroned
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the queen (Luna) enthroned.

## Iconography
A crowned queen, often robed in white or silver and associated with the moon, sits or stands in state within the architectural frame.

## Significance
The Queen is *Luna*, the lunar feminine principle — the white tincture, the philosophical silver, partner to the solar King. Her appearance marks the turn toward the *albedo*, the whitening, when the washed matter shines like the moon. She is the bride awaiting the chymical wedding.

## For artists & game designers
A moon-crowned queen in silver — the lunar counterpart figure for a paired king-and-queen design.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the king (Sol) enthroned
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the king (Sol) enthroned.

## Iconography
A crowned king in red or gold majesty, associated with the sun, enthroned within the painted border.

## Significance
The King is *Sol*, the solar masculine principle — the red tincture, philosophical gold, the perfected fixed body that is the goal of the Work. His enthronement images the *rubedo*, the reddening, in which the Stone attains its royal, tinting power. King and Queen together are sulphur and mercury, the chymical bridegroom and bride.

## For artists & game designers
A sun-crowned king in royal red — the solar half of the alchemical royal pair.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the hermaphrodite (Rebis)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the hermaphrodite (Rebis).

## Iconography
A two-headed, dual-bodied figure — male on one side, female on the other — stands crowned in the border, often holding emblems of sun and moon.

## Significance
The *Rebis* ('two-thing') is the union of opposites: Sol and Luna, sulphur and mercury, fixed and volatile fused into a single androgynous body. It is the *coniunctio*, the chymical wedding consummated, and one of alchemy's master-images for wholeness reconciled out of duality. Jung read it as the symbol of the integrated self.

## For artists & game designers
The classic two-headed Rebis — an immediately legible 'union of opposites' icon for a transformation reward screen.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — flask: the planetary stages (a sun-and-moon vessel)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to flask: the planetary stages (a sun-and-moon vessel).

## Iconography
A great glass flask or round-bottomed vessel dominates the page, its bulb holding a miniature scene — in the cycle a sequence of seven such flasks carries the Work through its planetary stages, each globe a little painted world of sun, moon, birds or figures.

## Significance
The seven flasks of the *Splendor Solis* map the operation onto the seven planets and seven metals, the matter ripening from Saturn-lead to Sol-gold. Each globe is a sealed cosmos — the philosophical egg in which death, washing and rebirth unfold under the eye of a planetary ruler. This is the treatise's most original contribution to the alchemical picture-book.

## For artists & game designers
A round flask whose glass bulb frames a tiny self-contained world — a perfect 'snow-globe' device for a stage-select interface.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the peacock's tail flask (cauda pavonis)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the peacock's tail flask (cauda pavonis).

## Iconography
A flask whose bulb shines with iridescent, peacock-feather colours — or a peacock itself displayed within the vessel — set in the architectural border.

## Significance
The *cauda pavonis*, the peacock's tail, is the shimmering play of colours that breaks out on the matter between the blackening and the whitening: a sign that the Work is alive and turning. The all-at-once iridescence promises that every colour, and so the perfected tincture, is latent in the stone.

## For artists & game designers
An iridescent peacock-tail shimmer inside glass — a gorgeous 'phase-shift' visual cue for a brewing minigame.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the white queen / white stone flask
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the white queen / white stone flask.

## Iconography
A flask or scene dominated by whiteness — a lunar woman in white, or a vessel showing the matter washed to a silvery brightness — within the border.

## Significance
The *albedo* perfected: the matter, washed clean of its blackness, shines white as the moon, yielding the white tincture that transmutes base metal to silver. The White Queen is the purified lunar body, ready to be reddened into the King.

## For artists & game designers
A luminous all-white vessel — a clean 'purified' state-icon contrasting the earlier blackening.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the red king / red stone flask
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the red king / red stone flask.

## Iconography
A flask or scene dominated by red — a solar man in red, or a vessel showing the matter glowing crimson — within the architectural frame.

## Significance
The *rubedo*, the reddening: the white stone is driven on to red, the perfected solar tincture that ripens base metal into gold. The Red King is the culminating fixed body, the Philosophers' Stone in its tinting glory — the end toward which every prior plate has pointed.

## For artists & game designers
A glowing crimson vessel — the triumphant 'gold achieved' state for a climactic reveal.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the sun child (filius solis)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the sun child (filius solis).

## Iconography
A radiant infant or youth, crowned or haloed with solar rays, born within or rising from the vessel — framed by the painted border.

## Significance
The *filius philosophorum*, the child of the Sun, is the reborn Stone: the new solar body delivered from the death and washing of the matter. The motif of the divine child crowns the death-and-rebirth drama of the whole cycle — the gold reborn as a living, luminous offspring.

## For artists & game designers
A glowing newborn sun-child — a luminous 'birth of the prize' moment for a culmination cutscene.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the moon child (filius lunae)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the moon child (filius lunae).

## Iconography
A pale infant or youth associated with the moon, born within the vessel, set in the architectural border.

## Significance
The lunar child is the white stone reborn — the silvery counterpart to the solar child, the purified body delivered from the *albedo*. Paired with the sun-child, it images the twin perfections, white and red, silver and gold, that the Work seeks.

## For artists & game designers
A pale, moon-touched infant — the lunar twin to the sun-child for paired birth imagery.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the alchemists at work in the laboratory
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the alchemists at work in the laboratory.

## Iconography
Men labour at a furnace and bench in a workshop — tending the athanor, working bellows, handling flasks — within the manuscript's border. In the *Splendor Solis* such genre scenes appear among the later leaves as small painted vignettes.

## Significance
These laboratory scenes ground the soaring allegory in real practice: the patient *coction*, the watched fire, the long tending of the vessel. They are a reminder that the Work was as much sweat and craft as symbol — the 'ora et labora' of the bench. Such vignettes are prized as documents of the early-modern alchemical workshop.

## For artists & game designers
A working alchemical lab with furnace, bellows and glassware — reference-grade staging for a crafting room.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the toiling figures (children's games / labours)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the toiling figures (children's games / labours).

## Iconography
A bustling scene of ordinary people at work and play — in the cycle the later leaves famously include vignettes of children's games, washing, and daily labour beneath emblematic skies, all within the painted border.

## Significance
These genre 'labours' encode the alchemical operations in the guise of common toil: washing, playing, building. The conceit — that the highest secret is mirrored in the humblest daily work, 'women's work and child's play' — is a recurring alchemical topos, insisting the Stone is made by simple, patient means.

## For artists & game designers
Lively everyday-labour vignettes under a symbolic sky — ambient world-building for an alchemical town.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the citadel / the philosophers' city
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the citadel / the philosophers' city.

## Iconography
A walled city or citadel rises in the background of a flask-scene or border — towers, gates and ramparts rendered with Renaissance precision.

## Significance
The philosophers' citadel images the completed, fortified Work — the Stone as a stronghold, the secret guarded behind walls. Architecture in the *Splendor Solis* is never idle: its painted buildings, like the elaborate borders themselves, frame the operations as a built, ordered cosmos achieved out of chaos.

## For artists & game designers
A Renaissance citadel as backdrop — a fortified 'secret city' vista for an alchemical realm.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — an illuminated leaf of the cycle (architectural border with allegory)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to an illuminated leaf of the cycle (architectural border with allegory).

## Iconography
A leaf of the manuscript given over to the *Splendor Solis*'s celebrated painted frame: an architectural border alive with putti, beasts, flowers, grotesques and antique ornament enclosing a central allegorical scene or vessel.

## Significance
The borders are not decoration but argument — each frames its plate with a teeming microcosm, binding the central operation to the whole order of nature. The *Splendor Solis* borders rank among the finest manuscript illumination of the German Renaissance, and carry their own emblematic burden of birds, fruit and figures that gloss the Work within.

## For artists & game designers
A richly grotesque illuminated frame — a luxurious border template to enclose any alchemical plate or UI panel.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the alchemist's coat of arms (frontispiece)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the alchemist's coat of arms (frontispiece).

## Iconography
A heraldic shield set within a painted architectural frame: the arms display a radiant golden sun — the *splendor solis*, the splendour of the Sun — that gives the treatise its name. Banderoles and a Latin or German motto frame the device, and grotesque, foliate and figural ornament crowds the border in the manner of a Renaissance armorial.

## Significance
The opening shield announces the whole programme: the Work aims at *Sol*, philosophical gold, the perfected solar tincture. By casting alchemy in the language of heraldry the manuscript dignifies the laboratory as a noble pursuit and binds the [[motif:solar|solar]] goal to lineage and honour. The arms are the emblematic 'title page' of one of the most beautiful of all alchemical books.

## For artists & game designers
A perfect arcane crest: a sun-in-splendour shield in an illuminated frame — drop-in heraldry for an alchemical order or guild.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the knight at the fountain (the philosopher)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the knight at the fountain (the philosopher).

## Iconography
An armoured knight or philosopher stands beside a flowing fountain, often holding a shield and a flask, with the spring of the *aqua permanens* (the permanent water, the philosophical mercury) welling at his feet. The scene is enclosed in the manuscript's signature painted architectural border teeming with figures, flora and fauna.

## Significance
The knight-philosopher guards the source of the alchemical water — the [[motif:mercury|mercurial]] spring from which the Work begins. He embodies the adept's vigilance and the chivalric framing of the quest for the Stone, a motif the *Splendor Solis* shares with the romance tradition. The fountain is the prima materia made to flow.

## For artists & game designers
An armoured guardian at a glowing spring — a ready quest-giver or threshold figure for an alchemical level.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the philosophical tree / two philosophers
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the philosophical tree / two philosophers.

## Iconography
Two robed philosophers confer before a tree or a fountain, one often presenting a golden vessel or pointing to a flask. The painted border frames them with putti, beasts and architectural fantasy.

## Significance
The conferring sages personify the transmission of secret knowledge — the Work is learned 'from mouth to mouth' between master and disciple. The tree is the [[motif:philosophical-tree|arbor philosophica]], whose growth images the maturing of metals toward gold. The plate sets the doctrinal frame before the operations begin.

## For artists & game designers
Two sages in debate beside a symbolic tree — instant lore-dialogue staging for a tutorial scene.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the drowning king rescued from the flood
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the drowning king rescued from the flood.

## Iconography
A crowned king flounders in water and is hauled to safety — a man on the bank reaches to draw him from the flood, set within the lavish architectural border.

## Significance
The drowning and rescue of the king is *solutio* and renewal: the old fixed body (gold, the king) must be 'killed' by dissolution in the philosophical water and then revived. It is one of the cycle's great images of death-and-rebirth, kin to the drowned kings of the *Rosarium* and *Aurora consurgens*. The flood is the mercurial bath that undoes and remakes the metallic body.

## For artists & game designers
A drowning crowned figure dragged from dark water — a dramatic rescue beat with built-in alchemical symbolism.

citations.text: Salomon Trismosin (attrib.), *Splendor Solis* (German illuminated MS, c. 1532–1535).
Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.
Internet Archive

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the king (Sol) enthroned
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the king (Sol) enthroned.

## Iconography
A crowned king in red or gold majesty, associated with the sun, enthroned within the painted border.

## Significance
The King is *Sol*, the solar masculine principle — the red tincture, philosophical gold, the perfected fixed body that is the goal of the Work. His enthronement images the *rubedo*, the reddening, in which the Stone attains its royal, tinting power. King and Queen together are sulphur and mercury, the chymical bridegroom and bride.

## For artists & game designers
A sun-crowned king in royal red — the solar half of the alchemical royal pair.

A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the hermaphrodite (Rebis).

## Iconography
A two-headed, dual-bodied figure — male on one side, female on the other — stands crowned in the border, often holding emblems of sun and moon.

## Significance
The *Rebis* ('two-thing') is the union of opposites: Sol and Luna, sulphur and mercury, fixed and volatile fused into a single androgynous body. It is the *coniunctio*, the chymical wedding consummated, and one of alchemy's master-images for wholeness reconciled out of duality. Jung read it as the symbol of the integrated self.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — flask: the planetary stages (a sun-and-moon vessel)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to flask: the planetary stages (a sun-and-moon vessel).

## Iconography
A great glass flask or round-bottomed vessel dominates the page, its bulb holding a miniature scene — in the cycle a sequence of seven such flasks carries the Work through its planetary stages, each globe a little painted world of sun, moon, birds or figures.

## Significance
The seven flasks of the *Splendor Solis* map the operation onto the seven planets and seven metals, the matter ripening from Saturn-lead to Sol-gold. Each globe is a sealed cosmos — the philosophical egg in which death, washing and rebirth unfold under the eye of a planetary ruler. This is the treatise's most original contribution to the alchemical picture-book.

## For artists & game designers
A round flask whose glass bulb frames a tiny self-contained world — a perfect 'snow-globe' device for a stage-select interface.

Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
J. K. Öbisch / Trismosin, *Splendor Solis* facsimile editions.

entries.title: *Splendor Solis* — the peacock's tail flask (cauda pavonis)
entries.creator: attrib. Salomon Trismosin (illuminator anonymous)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the *Splendor Solis*, the sumptuously illuminated German alchemical treatise attributed to Salomon Trismosin — of the kind the cycle devotes to the peacock's tail flask (cauda pavonis).

## Iconography
A flask whose bulb shines with iridescent, peacock-feather colours — or a peacock itself displayed within the vessel — set in the architectural border.

## Significance
The *cauda pavonis*, the peacock's tail, is the shimmering play of colours that breaks out on the matter between the blackening and the whitening: a sign that the Work is alive and turning. The all-at-once iridescence promises that every colour, and so the perfected tincture, is latent in the stone.

## For artists & game designers
An iridescent peacock-tail shimmer inside glass — a gorgeous 'phase-shift' visual cue for a brewing minigame.

Joscelyn Godwin (trans.) & Adam McLean (ed.), *Splendor Solis* (Phanes / Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
